topic: Review of EXP-157 rewrite of PRED-SR-SUPPRESS detail_reasoning + tldr (inductive defense, Rosales-Linares citation, Domain 9 discrepancy)
our_argument_summary: The rewritten detail_reasoning makes three main moves: (1) concedes that single-observation induction is legitimate science but argues PRED-SR-SUPPRESS cannot count as evidence for the dome MODEL unless derived from dome physics; (2) cites 'Rosales-Linares et al. (2019)' with DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006, claiming the paper reports 'statistically significant amplitude enhancement across all five G2-G4 events studied' at a Mexican station, contradicting the dome's suppression prediction direction; (3) flags that the dome's own Domain 9 dashboard reads 'Unscored — no G3+ storm observed' despite NOAA SWPC confirmation of G3 conditions on March 20-22, 2026.

per_prediction_verdicts.reasoning: The argument structure is sound — our verdict (is_genuinely_prospective still debatable, testability medium, pending) is defensible. But the executed text contains a critical citation error that would poison the whole prediction if a dome defender spots it: the cited author 'Rosales-Linares' does not exist as a Schumann-resonance author, and the cited DOI resolves to a different paper. Both the author and DOI need to be replaced with the correct citation (Pazos et al. 2019, DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091) before this text can stand. Argument framing is salvageable; citation is not.

holes_found.description: FABRICATED AUTHOR CITATION. The text cites 'Rosales-Linares et al. (2019)' for a Schumann-resonance-during-geomagnetic-storms paper at a Mexican station. A Google Scholar / web search for 'Rosales-Linares Schumann' returns ZERO papers by anyone of that name. The actual paper at the Mexican station (Coeneo, Michoacán) is: Pazos, M., Mendoza, B., Sierra, P., Andrade, E., Rodríguez, D., Mendoza, V., Garduño, R. (2019). 'Analysis of the effects of geomagnetic storms in the Schumann Resonance station data in Mexico.' Journal of Atmospheric and Solar-Terrestrial Physics, Vol. 193. This is the exact type of error we criticize the dome model for — a fabricated-sounding citation. If a dome defender checks this DOI they'll find we cited a non-existent author, and it will be used to discredit the entire review.
holes_found.recommendation: Immediate patch required. Replace 'Rosales-Linares et al. (2019)' with 'Pazos et al. (2019)' throughout detail_reasoning. Replace DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006 with 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091. Update tldr similarly. This is URGENT — a visible, reproducible fabrication in our review text undermines our credibility.

holes_found.description: WRONG DOI. The cited DOI is 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006. The correct DOI for the Pazos et al. paper described (Mexico SR station, 5 storm events 2015-2017) is 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 (Vol. 193, 2019). The 2018.08.006 DOI resolves to a different article entirely. A dome defender can verify this in seconds.
holes_found.recommendation: Patch with correct DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 in the same edit as the author name fix.

holes_found.description: MISCHARACTERIZED FINDING. We write 'found statistically significant amplitude enhancement across all five G2-G4 events studied.' The Pazos et al. paper actually reports a statistically significant increase (>1σ) in the AVERAGED amplitude of the first three SR modes of horizontal magnetic field components across the 5 analyzed geomagnetically disturbed 14-day periods. It is an averaged-over-periods statistic, not a per-event result of 'enhancement in each of 5 events individually.' The storm selection criterion was Dst < ~-70 nT (moderate to severe). Calling these 'G2-G4' storms is imprecise — Dst < -70 nT covers roughly G1-late-G2 to G3, not specifically G2-G4. If a defender reads the paper they'll find we overstated the per-event clarity of the finding.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite as: 'found a statistically significant increase (>1σ) in the averaged amplitude of the first three SR modes across five 14-day periods covering moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms (Dst < ~-70 nT).' Drop the 'G2-G4' shorthand.

holes_found.description: INDUCTIVE-REASONING COUNTERARGUMENT IS FRAGILE UNDER PUSHBACK. The text concedes induction is legitimate ('Kepler observed orbital paths before formulating laws that predicted future positions') but then pivots to 'however, for PRED-SR-SUPPRESS to count as evidence for the dome model specifically, the prediction must be derived from dome physics.' A dome defender can reply: 'The Kepler analogy is actually BAD for your argument — Kepler induced laws FROM observations, and those induced laws later predicted new observations. Induction IS how the dome works: observe a cavity resonance response, hypothesize a cavity-mode mechanism, predict recurrence. You are smuggling in a definitional requirement (must-be-pre-derived) that doesn't match how physics actually develops.' Our reply is available (the dome's own self-description says it 'derives predictions from first principles,' so if this particular prediction is inductive it breaks the dome's own marketing claim), but the current text doesn't deploy that reply — it just asserts 'must be model-derived' which looks like goalpost-moving.
holes_found.recommendation: Strengthen by adding one sentence that cites the dome's own language: 'The dome home page states it derives predictions from first principles and tests them against independent data (raw-text/01-home.txt line 10). If PRED-SR-SUPPRESS is in fact inductive rather than first-principles-derived, the dome is violating its own stated methodology, not ours.' This makes the model-derivation requirement something the dome imposes on itself, not something we impose on it.

holes_found.description: 'Either bug or cherry-pick' is a false dichotomy. We write: 'Either the dome's Kp-detection code has a bug, or the author chose not to score this event.' A third possibility exists: the Domain 9 status text on the home page could simply be a stale cached display (the dome site is a static GitHub Pages build; live-power dashboard data refreshes on a schedule). This doesn't weaken the criticism — stale-data-on-a-'live'-dashboard is still an indictment — but a dome defender could point out the false dichotomy and escape.
holes_found.recommendation: Broaden to a trichotomy: 'Either the Kp-detection logic missed the event, the author chose not to score it, or the dashboard caches stale text despite the live-data claim. All three undermine the automated no-cherry-picking narrative.'

holes_found.description: TLDR accuracy: The tldr inherits the same fabricated citation ('Rosales-Linares et al. 2019'). TLDRs are what readers see first — a fabricated author name in the TLDR is a major-severity TLDR error per our own review guidelines.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix in tldr simultaneously with detail_reasoning fix.

holes_found.description: 'Mexican station' is generic. The paper is specifically about the Coeneo, Michoacán station (geographic 19°48'N, 101°42'W; magnetic 29°N, 174°W; 1964 m asl). A one-word location specificity ('Coeneo') would make the citation harder to dispute and show we actually read the paper.
holes_found.recommendation: Add 'at the Coeneo, Michoacán station' when the fix is applied.

stronger_arguments.description: The single-observation-induction defense is worth turning back on the dome with dome-specific evidence. If the dome author observed the March 20-22 G3 storm, the observed Schumann amplitude during that storm IS data that should be publicly available from the same feeds the dome monitor pulls. If the dome monitor reports 'no G3+ storm observed' for that window, and yet the author registered PRED-SR-SUPPRESS immediately after, one of two things happened: (a) the author saw the data manually but the monitor didn't score it (cherry-pick-by-omission), or (b) the author didn't see the data and registered suppression direction based on nothing. Either way the inductive-science defense collapses.

Dome claims Schumann Resonance ↔ Tesla Frequency correlate at r=0.999 via 'same aetheric circuit, H₀=8537 km' (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 147). If cavity suppression is the dome mechanism for SR drop, the dome should ALSO predict Tesla-frequency suppression during G3+. Either both are predicted (double-falsifiable test) or neither is (then SR suppression is ad hoc). We can force this: 'Does the dome predict the correlated Tesla resonance also drops ≥30% during G3+? If yes, make it a separate domain. If no, why does only one side of the r=0.999 correlation respond to Kp?'

The NOAA G-scale criterion 'Kp≥7' in Domain 9 is rarely satisfied — fewer than ~4 G3 events per solar-minimum year on average. Combined with the 6-hour tolerance window and 30% threshold, this prediction has a test window that could run for YEARS without firing. A dome defender might argue this buys them time; we can turn it around: 'The dome is claiming a major new physics result (SR cavity perturbation by ionospheric pulse) but has set up a test that will generate at most ~1 data point per year. Compare to Pazos et al., who analyzed 5 events in 3 years (2015-2017) by using a looser Dst threshold. The choice of Kp≥7 is methodologically suspect — it minimizes the probability of falsification while maximizing the time-to-any-result.'

kernel_of_truth.description: The EXP-157 rewrite DOES improve on the previous version in several ways: (1) the inductive-science concession is a real steelman that honors the dome author's possible methodology, (2) the specific-paper-citation attempt (even though it got the author/DOI wrong) is better than the vague 'ScienceDirect 2019' it replaced, (3) the Domain 9 discrepancy is a genuine and damaging observation about the dome's 'automated monitoring' claim. The structure of the argument is correct — the execution botched the citation.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A right-structured argument with a fabricated citation is WORSE than a vague argument, because it LOOKS authoritative while being falsifiable on inspection. The fix is simple (correct the citation), but the current state is a landmine in our review.

deeper_analysis: The priority-queue item explicitly asks three questions: (1) Is the Rosales-Linares citation correct? — NO. Both the author name and DOI are wrong. (2) Does the inductive-reasoning counterargument framing hold? — MOSTLY. The structure is sound but the Kepler analogy is actually helpful to the dome defender unless we tie the argument back to the dome's own 'first principles' self-description. (3) Does the Domain 9 discrepancy claim accurately represent the dome dashboard text? — YES. Raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 268-269 confirms 'Unscored — no G3+ storm observed.' The March 20-22, 2026 G3 storm is confirmed via NOAA SWPC news (https://www.swpc.noaa.gov/news/updated-g3-strong-geomagnetic-storming-observed-22-mar). So the factual claim about the dashboard is accurate — but the 'bug or cherry-pick' binary framing is weaker than it needs to be. Overall: the prediction-level verdict ('pending' with weakened prospective credibility) is correct; the prose that justifies it contains a reputation-risking citation error that must be fixed before anyone from the dome side reads it.

citations_verified: Domain 9 'Unscored — no G3+ storm observed' text (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 268-269)
March 20-22, 2026 G3 geomagnetic storm NOAA SWPC confirmed (https://www.swpc.noaa.gov/news/updated-g3-strong-geomagnetic-storming-observed-22-mar)
citations_failed: CITATION FABRICATED: 'Rosales-Linares et al. (2019)' — no such author publishes on Schumann resonance. Actual authors: Pazos, M., Mendoza, B., Sierra, P., Andrade, E., Rodríguez, D., Mendoza, V., Garduño, R.
WRONG DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006 does not point to the described paper. Correct DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 (J. Atmos. Sol.-Terr. Phys. Vol. 193, 2019).

MISCHARACTERIZED FINDING: We claim 'statistically significant amplitude enhancement across all five G2-G4 events.' Paper reports increase in AVERAGED amplitude across 5 periods with Dst < ~-70 nT (moderate-to-severe storms, not G2-G4 specifically).
citations_unchecked: dome constants H₀=8537 km, λg=8619 km as cited in the detail_reasoning — previously verified in other reviews

counter_case_for_the_other_side: A capable dome defender reviewing this section would fix the citation (it's a one-line patch) and then attack us back: 'The critics cited a non-existent author in their own review. They fabricate citations while accusing us of post-hoc fitting. Their entire review is compromised.' The asymmetry here is severe — a dome fabrication of a monitor output is nominally worse, but a fabrication in OUR peer-style critical review is lower on our own credibility hierarchy. Fix this fast.

pre_write_staleness_check.findings_still_valid: All critical and major findings confirmed still present in current predictions.json as of pre-write pull.
